title: One variable, many values
instruction:
Imagine you want to store five student names. Without lists, you'd do this:

```python
student1 = "Alice"
student2 = "Bob"
student3 = "Carol"
student4 = "David"
student5 = "Eve"
```

And if you want to loop over them? Nope, you can't. What if you add more students? More variables.

Lists solve this completely: **one variable, as many values as you want.**

`students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]`

Run this.
successMessage: One variable holds all five. len() counts them. Try adding more names to the list — the code doesn't change at all. That's the whole point.

title: Getting items by position
instruction:
Every item in a list has a position called an **index**. Python starts counting at 0:

```
["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
   [0]     [1]    [2]
```

And Python's best trick: **negative indexes count from the back**.

`students[-1]` is always the last item — no matter how long the list is.

Print the first, third, and last student.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
# TODO: print first, third, and last student

codeChecks.message: Access first item: students[0]
Access third item: students[2] (remember: 0 is first, 2 is third)
Access last item: students[-1]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Index 0 = first, -1 = always last. These two are used constantly. Especially students[-1] — it works on any list regardless of size.

title: Changing items
instruction:
Lists are **mutable** — you can change items after creating the list. Just assign to the index:

`students[1] = "Robert"` — replaces whatever was at position 1.

Change 'Bob' to 'Robert' and change 'Eve' (the last one) to 'Emma'.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: change Bob to Robert and Eve to Emma
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Change students[1] to "Robert"
Change the last item to "Emma"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Bob is Robert, Eve is Emma. Lists remember every change you make. This mutability is what makes lists so powerful for building up and adjusting collections of data.

title: append() and insert()
instruction:
Two ways to add items:

- `list.append(item)` — adds to the **end** (fast)
- `list.insert(index, item)` — adds at a specific **position** (pushes everything else right)

Append 'Frank' to the end. Then insert 'Zara' at position 0 (making her first).
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: append Frank and insert Zara at position 0
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Append Frank: students.append("Frank")
Insert Zara at position 0: students.insert(0, "Zara")
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: append adds to the end instantly (O(1) — constant time). insert pushes everything to make room (O(n) — slower on big lists). For building up lists, always prefer append.

title: remove() and pop()
instruction:
Two ways to remove items:

- `list.remove(value)` — finds and removes the **first occurrence** of that value
- `list.pop(index)` — removes by **position** and **gives you back** the removed item
- `list.pop()` — removes the last item

Remove 'Bob' by value. Then pop the last item (and you can store it if you want it).
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: remove Bob, then pop the last item
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Remove Bob: students.remove("Bob")
Pop the last item: students.pop()
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: remove by value, pop by index. Both modify the list in-place. pop returns the removed item — so if you want to know what you removed, save it: last = students.pop()

title: Slicing — grab a portion
instruction:
Slicing extracts a portion of a list without changing the original:

- `list[start:stop]` — from start up to (not including) stop
- `list[:3]` — first 3 items
- `list[-2:]` — last 2 items
- `list[::2]` — every other item

Extract the first three students, the last two, and every other student.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
# TODO: print first 3, last 2, and every other student

codeChecks.message: Slice first 3: students[:3]
Slice last 2: students[-2:]
Every other item: students[::2]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Slicing creates a NEW list — the original is untouched. This is one of Python's most distinctive features. Slicing works on strings too: "hello"[:3] gives "hel".

title: Useful list methods
instruction:
Lists come with a toolbox of built-in methods:

- `sorted(list)` — returns a new sorted list (original unchanged)
- `list.sort()` — sorts the list in-place (original changed)
- `list.count(x)` — how many times does x appear?
- `list.index(x)` — what position is x at?

Run the starter to see sort vs sorted. Then add: count how many 3s there are, and find the index of 7.
starter:
numbers = [5, 2, 8, 3, 1, 7, 3, 9]
print(sorted(numbers))
print(numbers)  # original unchanged
numbers.sort()
print(numbers)  # now sorted in-place
# TODO: count 3s and find index of 7

codeChecks.message: Count 3s: numbers.count(3)
Find index of 7: numbers.index(7)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: sorted() creates a copy, sort() changes the original. Know which you want before you use it — getting them mixed up causes subtle bugs. Ask yourself: 'do I want to keep the original?'

title: in — is it there?
instruction:
From Chapter 6 you know `in` works on strings. It works on lists too:

`"Alice" in students` → True if Alice is in the list.

Check which subjects from a wishlist are actually available.
starter:
available_subjects = ["Math", "Science", "History", "English"]
wanted = ["Math", "Art", "Science"]

# TODO: for each subject in wanted, print whether it is available
# "Math: available" or "Art: not available"

codeChecks.message: Use: if subject in available_subjects:
Loop over wanted: for subject in wanted:
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Membership check on a list. Note: for small lists this is fine. For thousands of items, a set gives you the same in but much faster. You'll learn sets later.

title: List comprehension with a filter
instruction:
Remember list comprehensions from Chapter 7? You can add a condition to filter the results:

`[x for x in numbers if x > 0]` — only keeps positive numbers

Create two filtered lists:
1. All even numbers from 1–20
2. Students with names longer than 4 characters
hint:
[x for x in range(1, 21) if x % 2 == 0]
[s for s in students if len(s) > 4]
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve", "Isabella"]

# TODO: list of even numbers 1-20
evens = []
print(evens)

# TODO: list of students with names longer than 4 characters
long_names = []
print(long_names)

codeChecks.message: Use comprehension with filter: [x for x in range(1,21) if x % 2 == 0]
Filter by name length: [s for s in students if len(s) > 4]
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Filtered comprehensions. This pattern — create a new list by transforming and filtering another — is Python's most idiomatic way to process data.

title: Build a grade tracker
instruction:
Final challenge — let's build something real.

Create a list of 5 scores (make them up). Then:
1. Print the highest score with `max()`
2. Print the lowest score with `min()`
3. Calculate and print the average (use `sum()` and `len()`)
4. Print all scores above 85 using list comprehension

No starter code. You have all the pieces.
hint:
avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
high_scores = [s for s in scores if s > 85]

codeChecks.message: Use max() for the highest score.
Use min() for the lowest score.
Use sum() for the total (needed for average).
Use list comprehension with a condition for the high scores.

successMessage: A real grade tracker! max, min, average, filtered list — all in less than 10 lines. Lists are Python's most used data structure. You now know them inside and out. Chapter 8 done!
